sourceMovie.title: Alice in Wonderland
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Audiences latched onto Alice in Wonderland (2010) as a family theatrical experience: big enough for a group watch, specific enough to reward a second viewing once you know where it's headed. The family and fantasy blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Charlie and the Chocolate Factory
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Alice in Wonderland clicked for you, Charlie and the Chocolate Factory (2005) is a strong next stop: both trade in family and fantasy, with comic timing and sharper banter. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.title: Maleficent
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Alice in Wonderland clicked for you, Maleficent (2014) is a strong next stop: both trade in family and fantasy, with earned warmth without mush. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.title: The Lord of the Rings: The Fellowship of the Ring
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Alice in Wonderland clicked for you, The Lord of the Rings: The Fellowship of the Ring (2001) is a strong next stop: both trade in fantasy and adventure, with momentum and clean stakes. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

faq.question: What should I watch after Alice in Wonderland?
faq.answer: Try Charlie and the Chocolate Factory, Maleficent, The Jungle Book—each shares DNA with Alice in Wonderland while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Alice in Wonderland?
faq.answer: Alice in Wonderland (2010) is best described as Family, Fantasy, Adventure. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Alice in Wonderland part of a franchise?
faq.answer: Yes—it's part of the "Alice in Wonderland Collection" storyline. You can enjoy it standalone, but sequels and tie-ins build on characters and continuity established here.
